coredata.dc:title: Concentration-dependent color tunability of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and their application for iron(III) detection and multicolor bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chan
Hu, Tantan
Wen, Zhuoqi
Zhou, Jingdan
Wang, Xioajie
Wu, Qian
Wang, Chuanxi
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doping can effectly adjust the compositions and structures of carbon dots and hence enhance their fluorescence. In this work, we report a fast and low-cost route for synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by microwave pyrolysis of citric acid and ammonium within 7 min. The as-prepared N-CDs contain plentiful oxygen and nitrogen functional groups, and dispaly intense fluorescence with high quantum yield of ca. 44.3% and have an average size of 1.8 nm. The obtained N-CDs exhibit highly stable against photobleaching, ionic strengths, and can be used for selective and sensitive detection of Fe(III). It is postulated that the Fe3+-mediated fluorescence quenching is attributed to the charge transfer between N-CDs and Fe3+. In particular, the emission peaks from blue to red region can be tuned by interparticle distance of N-CDs, simply by increasing the concentration of N-CDs in aqueous solution, which indicates its potential applications as a promising optical image probe in multicolor cellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Multicolor fluorescence
High quantum yield
Concentration-dependent fluorescence
Biosensing and cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental procedure for the selectivity and sensitivity of Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) schematic diagram for synthesizing N-CDs; (b) UV–Vis absorption (Abs), excitation (ex) and emission (em) spectra of resultant N-CDs in aqueous solution (0.78 mg/mL); Inset: photographs of N-CDs aqueous solution taken under UV (365 nm) irradiation and room light, respectively; (c) TEM image of the N-CDs (0.78 mg/mL) and high-resolution TEM image (Inset) of the N-CDs; (d) the size distribution measured for 100 N-CDs randomly.
XPS spectra of as-prepared N-CDs (0.78 mg/mL): (a) Survey spectrum; (b) C 1s spectrum; (c) O 1s spectrum; (d) N 1s spectrum.

Concentration effect on PL of the N-CDs: PL spectra of the N-CDs with various concentrations of (a) 0.78, (b) 2.08, (c) 2.60 and (d) 10.42 mg/mL under different excitation wavelengths; (e) Normalized PL spectra of N-CDs with different concentrations excited at maximum excitation wavelength; (f) Emission images of the N-CDs with different concentrations (bottom) excited at maximum excitation wavelength and corresponding images under room light.

(a) Photo stability of the N-CDs irradiated by a 450 W xenon lamp at various time; (b) PL intensity of the N-CDs in various pH values; (c) PL intensity of the N-CDs in different concentrations of NaCl aqueous solution. The concentration of the CDs was 0.78 mg/mL, and the intensity is evaluated by the emission at 450 nm.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of N-CDs (0.31 mg/mL) in the presence of Fe3+ ions (0–500 µM), and the inset is thefluorescent photographs of aqueous solutions of N-CDs without (left) and with (right) 80 μM of Fe3+; (b) The change of PL intensity of N-CDs solution versus the concentration of Fe3+; (c) Comparison of PL intensity of the N-CDs (0.31 mg/mL) after adding different metal ions (100 μM); (d) Fluorescence decay of N-CDs without and with Fe3+ (80 μM) as a function of time (360 nm excitation, detected at 450 nm emission).
Confocal fluorescent images of HeLa cells incubating with CDs at different concentrations: (a), 0.5 mg/mL; (b), 2.1 mg/mL; (c), 10.5 mg/mL.

head.ce:title: Concentration-dependent color tunability of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and their application for iron(III) detection and multicolor bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical & Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Energy Technology, Ningbo Institute of Industrial Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Public Health Research Center, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of New Energy Technology, Ningbo Institute of Industrial Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, PR China (C. Wang).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The N-doped CDs with high quantum yield and stability are synthesized by rapid microwave route. As-prepared CDs exhibit multicolor fluorescence via adjusting their concentrations and could be used for biosensing and bioimaging.

Nitrogen doping can effectly adjust the compositions and structures of carbon dots and hence enhance their fluorescence. In this work, we report a fast and low-cost route for synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by microwave pyrolysis of citric acid and ammonium within 7 min. The as-prepared N-CDs contain plentiful oxygen and nitrogen functional groups, and dispaly intense fluorescence with high quantum yield of ca. 44.3% and have an average size of 1.8 nm. The obtained N-CDs exhibit highly stable against photobleaching, ionic strengths, and can be used for selective and sensitive detection of Fe(III). It is postulated that the Fe3+-mediated fluorescence quenching is attributed to the charge transfer between N-CDs and Fe3+. In particular, the emission peaks from blue to red region can be tuned by interparticle distance of N-CDs, simply by increasing the concentration of N-CDs in aqueous solution, which indicates its potential applications as a promising optical image probe in multicolor cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: As a group of newly emerged fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) are superior to semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes because of their high solubility, robust chemical inertness, high resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and facile modification [1–4], making them great potential in bioimaging [5], photocatalysis [6,7], fluorescence sensors [8], optronics [9,10], etc. In the past decades, many methods have been developed to prepare CDs, including electrochemical oxidation [11], chemical oxidation [12], microwave assisted synthesis [13,14], hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment [15,16] and ultrasonic methods [17], etc. Reports on the preparation of CDs are gradually mature, and more and more studies seek to pursue simple and cost-effective methods to obtain CDs with high quality. Among them, the synthesis by microwave pyrolysis of carbohydrate precursors provides rapid heating to the reaction medium and requires no high temperature or high pressure. The low-cost and energy-saving technique has attracted much attention due to the short consumption time and moderate synthetic condition [18,19].

The most fascinating feature of CDs is their tunable photoluminescence (PL) property. In this field, many efforts have been put into tune the photoluminescence of CDs and obtain multi-emission colors, which is crucial for multicolor patterning, light emitting diodes, and bioimaging applications [13,20–22]. Hence, it is desirable and highly worthy to develop effect approach for acquiring CDs with tunable multicolor luminescence. However, the key to obtain CDs with tunable emission is the understanding of luminescence mechanism. Generally, the tunable emissions of CDs can be assigned to a result of quantum confinement of conjugated π-electrons in sp2 carbon network (size-dependent emission) and surface-related defective sites (surface state-dependent emission), even though the exact origins of their tunable PL property remain debatable and systematic research is needed to paint a clearer picture of the mechanisms [23,24]. Therefore, various approaches including heteroatom doping [25], surface passivation [26], and size control [27] have been reported to control the composition or the size of CDs, eventually tune their PL emission. However, only a few studies have been reported on concentration-dependent emission of CDs [28], which means the tunable PL emission can be achieved only by adjusting the concentration of CDs.

In addition, heteroatom doping is usually found to be an effect way to adjust the compositions and structures of CDs and hence enhance their fluorescence [29–31]. For instance, Wei et al. prepared a series of N-doped CDs with quantum yields (QYs) up to 69.1% [30]; Qu et al. obtained the highest PLQYs (94%) of N-doped CDs using citric acid as a carbon source and ethylene diamine as a N source [31]. The N enters the graphene framework through intramolecular dehydrolysis and the extent of doping effectively improves the QY of N-doped CDs. Despite several successes in this area, it is still of great urgency to develop simple and effective methods to synthesize N-doped CDs with high QYs. Herein, we present a fast and low-cost microwave route to prepare N-doped CDs (N-CDs) using citric acid (CA) and ammonia as sources. Our results show that the resultant N-CDs contain plentiful oxygen and nitrogen functional groups, exhibit intense PL with the high QYs of ca. 44.3%. Most interestingly, the as-prepared N-CDs display concentration-dependent emission. The PL emission from red to blue can be tuned by interparticle distance, which are achieved simply by diluting aqueous solution of the N-CDs. With dilution, the PL spectra are not only blue-shifted but also the intensity increases followed by a decrease in the concentration. Furthermore, the as-prepared N-doped CDs are applied as nanosensor probes for Fe3+ detection and multicolor cellular imaging.

Citric acid (CA), ammonia and acetone were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The solution of Fe3+ was prepared from FeCl3. The solutions of other metal ions were prepared from their nitrate, acetate, or chloride salts, and freshly prepared before using. The reagents above were of analytical reagent grade and used as received without further purification. De-ionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

CA (1.0 g) was dissolved in 21 mL of water, and mixed with ammonia (9.34 mL) in a conical flask with stirring for 1 min. The mixture was then put at the center of the rotation plate of a domestic microwave oven (700 W) for about 7 min. The solution changed from transparent to dark brown, indicating the formation of N-CDs. After cooling to room temperature, 10 mL of DI water was poured into conical flask to dissolve the product and obtained brown and transparent N-CDs solution. The clear brown solution was utilized for further purification.

For further purification of synthesized N-CDs, crude N-CDs aqueous solution (10 mL) was poured into 5 times volume amount of acetone solvent and stirred for 4 h. Then, the mixture underwent centrifuge and rotary evaporation to remove all the organic solvent. The remaining N-CDs were re-dissolved in DI water, which was called purified N-CDs aqueous solution. The N-CDs could be dissolved in organic solvent which was miscible with water. We then compared QYs of the N-CDs before and after the purification procedure. It turned out that the purified N-CDs had a higher QYs of 44.3% than that of rough N-CDs (20.6%). Finally, the purified N-CDs were freeze-dried and then stored in the dark for further use.

The as-prepared N-CDs were prepared to different concentrations (e.g., 0.78, 2.08, 2.60, and 10.42 mg/mL) in water. For example, the 0.78 mg/mL of N-CDs aqueous solution was prepared by dissolving 7.8 mg of N-CDs powder in 10 mL of DI water. Then, the PL spectra of the N-CDs aqueous solution with different concentrations were recorded by fluorescence spectrofluorimeter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental procedure for the selectivity and sensitivity of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: 0.31 mg/ml of as-prepared N-CDs aqueous solution was prepared for further use. To test the fluorescence response of the N-CDs on several metallic ions, many different kinds of metal cations were chosen for the detection, including NaCl, KNO3, K2CrO4, ZnCl2, Ba(NO3)2, FeCl3, Ca(NO3)2, MgSO4, Cu(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, CH3COO)3Cr, PbCl2, Cd(NO3)2, CO(NO3)2 and (CH3COO)2Mn. Aqueous solutions of these metal salts (1 mM) were all freshly prepared. Typically, 1.80 mL of the diluted N-CDs solution above was mixed with one of metal salt (1 mM, 0.2 mL) aqueous solution. The fluorescence measurement was performed after 5 min. To investigate the sensitivity of the N-CDs (0.31 mg/ml) to Fe3+, FeCl3 aqueous solution (0.20 mL) with certain concentration was added to 1.80 mL of diluted N-CDs solution. Then the fluorescence measurement was carried out after stirring for 5 min.

The vitro cellular uptake of N-CDs in HeLa cells was examined at different concentrations of N-CDs ions by confocal laser scanning microscopy, which was similar as previous reports [4,14,15]. In typical process, HeLa cells (210 cells/mL) were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicil-lin/streptomycin (DMEM) using 96-well plate. Suspensions (0.5, 2.1, and 10.5 mg/mL) of N-CDs from the stock solution were prepared with Dulbecco’s phosphate buffer saline (DPBS). After sonication for 10 min to ensure complete dispersion, an aliquot (typically 0.01 mL) of the suspension was added to the well of a chamber slide containing the cells cultured for 12 h. The chamber slide was then incubated at 37 °C in a CO2 incubator for 12 h for N-CDs uptake. Prior to fixation of the cells on the slide for inspection with a confocal fluorescence microscope, the excess N-CDs were removed by washing 3 times with warm DPBS.

UV–Vis absorption spectrum was obtained by using a TU–1991 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Puxi Inc., China). Photoluminescence (PL) experiments were performed with a Cary Esclipse spectrofluorimeter (Varian, America). X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using Mg Kα excitation (1253.6 eV) was collected in a VG ESCALAB MKII spectrometer (VG, Britain). Binding energy calibration was based on C 1s at 284.6 eV. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured at wavenumbers ranging from 500 to 4000 cm−1 using a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The morphology and mean diameter of resultant CDs were characterized by a JEM–2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). The confocal microscopy images were taken at Olympus Fluoview FV1000. All measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions.

Microwave-assisted technique is an effective route for gaining nanoparticles [18,19,32]. It provides efficient and fast heating process to the reaction mixture, significantly shortening the reaction times. Compared to conventional methods, it is a low-cost and energy-saving technique and requires no high temperature or high pressure in the preparation of nanoparticles. The N-doped CDs (N-CDs) were prepared by a microwave pyrolysis method using citric acid (CA) and aqueous ammonia as sources. CA provides a carbon source, and ammonia acts as doping agent. The synthetic procedure is depicted in Fig. 1 a. Typically, CA and aqueous ammonia were added to DI water to form a well-distributed solution. Then, the solution was heated in a domestic 700 W microwave oven for 7 min. The resulting crude product was extracted by acetone solvent and the purified N-CDs were obtained by rotary evaporation (for details see the experimental part) [33]. The synthesized N-CDs were examined for their optical properties. As shown in Fig. 1b, the UV–Vis spectrum display two clear absorption bands at 237 nm and 333 nm, which is similar to previously reported CDs [34–36]. The origin of these peaks is related to π electron transition in oxygen-containing N-CDs. The peak at 237 nm is typically ascribed to π-π* transition of CC bond in aromatic sp2 domains [35] and the peak at 337 nm corresponds to n-π* transition of CO bond [36], indicating the presence of carbonyl or conjugated carbonyl groups. Upon the maximum excitation at 360 nm, the N-CDs in aqueous solution exhibit blue fluorescence with λmax at 450 nm and the full width at half maximum around 95 nm (Fig. 1b). The diluted solution of the as-prepared N-CDs is slight yellow color under visible light, while it emits intense blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light illumination (Inset of Fig. 1b). The size and morphology of N-CDs were confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As displayed in Fig. 1c, the N-CDs were well mono-dispersed and uniform in size. As estimated from the TEM image, the as-synthesized N-CDs have a narrow size distribution in the range from 0.7 to 2.5 nm, with an average size of 1.8 nm (Fig. 1d). In addition, lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is observed in the corresponding high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 1c), which is attributed to the d spacings of the graphene (100) planes [37]. The large-scale TEM and HRTEM images of N-CDs are shown in Fig. S1. Quantum yield (QY) is a significant parameter to characterize photoluminescence (PL). Moreover, exploration of PL carbon-based nanomaterials with high QYs (>30%), for accelerating their practical applications, such as fluorescent chemical sensing and bioimaging, still remains significant importance [31]. It is noted N-doping is an effect way to improve fluorescence of the CDs [29–31,38,39]. We then measured QY of the as-prepared N-CDs, which was calculated to be about 44.3% using quinine sulfate (54% in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4, λex = 360 nm) as a reference. This value is comparable to the previous researches in which N-doped CDs was found to enhance fluorescence dramatically [38,39].

The surface functional groups and composition of N-CDs were confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. As shown in Fig. S2, the peaks emerge at 3440 and 3160 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching modes of NH/OH. The peaks around 2930 and 2758 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of CH. The stretching vibration of CO is measured at 1715 cm−1, and it is found that the CC and CN stretching vibration bands are found at 1590 and 1400 cm−1 [40], respectively. The peaks at 1204 and 1074 cm−1 related to the CO stretching vibrations imply the existence of large numbers of O-containing groups [41]. The full range XPS analysis (Fig. 2 a) of the resultant N-CDs sample clearly show three peaks at 531.5, 400.0, and 287.5 eV, which are attributed to O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, respectively [34], confirming the presence of C, N and O in the N-CDs. The high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (b) can be well divided into four-unit moieties [38]: CC/CC with a binding energy at 284.5 eV; CO at 285.0 eV; CN at 286.5 eV; and CO/CN at 288.5 eV. And there are two peaks located at 531.6 and 532.3 eV in the high-resolution O 1s spectra (Fig. 2c), which arise from CO and COC/COH bands [39], respectively. The N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2d) around 399.6, 400.3, and 401.6 eV was pointed to the nitrogen atom of pyridinic-like N, pyrrolic-like N and NH groups [42], respectively. This indicates that element N is doped into the CDs successfully. The surface components of the N-CDs determined by the XPS are good agreement with FTIR results. It has demonstrated that the surface of the N-CDs is filled with hydrophilic oxygen functional groups and nitrogen functional groups. Moreover, XPS spectra along with FTIR analysis data of N-CDs confirmed that these N-CDs contained the elements N, C, O, and H with element N on the surface and in internal core, which is important for improving the PL of CDs.

Interestingly, the as-prepared N-CDs display concentration-dependent emission, which means the PL emission of the N-CDs can be tuned by the concentration. The N-CDs aqueous solution with concentration of 0.78, 2.08, 2.60 and 10.42 mg/mL were prepared respectively. We then investigated the concentration effect on PL of the N-CDs. Fig. 3 shows PL spectra of the N-CDs with various concentrations at several excitation wavelengths. As previous research, the N-CDs at various concentrations all exhibit typical excitation-dependent PL behavior [43,44], that is, the PL peak of N-CDs shifts to longer wavelength with the increase of excitation wavelength accompanied by a significant reduction in PL intensity. Moreover, as the concentration of the N-CDs increases, a huge red-shift in PL spectra is observed as shown in Fig. 3a–d. The fluorescence peaks clearly shift from 450 nm (0.78 mg/mL N-CDs in aqueous solution) to 523, 550 and 600 nm for 2.08, 2.60 and 10.42 mg/mL, respectively. Fig. 3e shows normalized PL spectra of Fig. 3a–d at maximum excitation wavelength, from which the shift of PL peak position from blue to red is clearly observed. In addition, the optical images of the N-CDs with different concentrations excited at maximum excitation wavelength as well as corresponding images under room light are shown in Fig. 3f. Therefore, simply by adjusting the concentration, we obtained multicolor photoluminescence of the N-CDs aqueous solutions in wide range.

Generally, the PL intensity of N-CDs decreases with increasing the concentration (Fig. 3a–d), which caused by reabsorption effect at high concentration. It has reported that the tunable emission of the CDs could be a result of different sizes distribution and the distribution of different emissive sites on the surface of CDs [23,24,45,46]. To gain more insight into the origin of the PL behavior and explain the concentration-dependent emission of the N-CDs, more information on surface chemistry and size distribution of N-CDs from low to high concentration was further provided. As shown in b, it is found that the N-CDs with high concentration (2.60 mg/mL, yellow luminescence) have the size distribution in the range from 0.7 to 2.2 nm with an average size of 1.1 nm, which coincide almost with result of N-CDs at low concentration (0.78 mg/mL, blue luminescence). Figs. S3 and S4 show XPS spectra and FTIR spectra of N-CDs with different concentrations. The XPS spectra of N-CDs with high concentration (10.42 mg/mL, red luminescence) are similar to the N-CDs with 1ow concentration. Besides, the resultant N-CDs display no change in the FTIR spectra upon increasing the concentration from 0.78 to 10.42 mg/mL (Fig. S4). The observed results so far suggests that the size distribution and composition groups on the surface of N-CDs at low or high concentration are nearly unchanged and played no part in the red shift caused by the increase of concentration. However, Nanda et al. demonstrated that the tunable emission of the N-CDs could be controlled by the interparticle distance [28]. At low concentration, the increase of interpartical distance decreases the interaction among the particles, resulting in the blue-shift with increase of PL intensity due to the radiative process. At higher concentration, the interparticle distance decreases and the interaction between the particles increases, resulting in the red-shift and decrease of PL intensity due to the self-absorption.

The stability of luminescent nanomaterials was an important factor to assess their applications [47,48]. We further investigated the influence of photo irradiation, pH and ionic strength to the PL intensity of the N-CDs (Fig. 4 ). As shown in Fig. 4a, the N-CDs was exposed under a 450 W xenon (Xe) lamp for various time spans to probe their photo-stability. The PL intensity of the N-CDs decreased only slightly and preserved ∼95% of the initial intensity even after 120 min irradiation by 450 W Xe lamp, indicating that the N-CDs are highly resistant to photobleaching. The PL spectra of the N-CDs irradiated by a 450 W xenon lamp at various time in detail were given in Fig. S5. However, different pH values had distinct effects on the PL intensity of the N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 4b. Under both strong acidic and alkaline conditions, the PL intensity were decreased dramatically. The emission intensity reached the maximum value when the pH was approximately 7.0. Fig. S6 displayed the PL spectra of the N-CDs responses to pH ranging systematically from 1.09 to 13.01 in detail. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4c, the PL intensity of the N-CDs was relatively constant with increasing concentrations of NaCl in aqueous solution up to 1 mol/L, suggesting high stability of the N-CDs even under high ionic-strength conditions. The detailed PL spectra of the N-CDs containing different concentrations of NaCl were given in Fig. S7. Therefore, the as-prepared N-CDs exhibited good stability in this article. We argued the functional groups on the surface of the N-CDs could improve the hydrophilicity and stability of the N-CDs, which is conducive to the applications of CDs in the area of chemical sensing.

Since the oxygen functional groups and nitrogen functional groups on the surface of N-CDs can interact with metal ions to form complex compounds by coordination, the resultant N-CDs provide a potential possibility to be used as a fluorescent probe for detection of metal ions. It was found that the as-prepared N-CDs are sensitive toward Fe3+, which is indispensable for metabolic processes of all living systems [49]. Fig. 5 a shows the Fluorescence quenching of N-CDs after adding various concentrations of Fe3+. With the increase of the concentration of Fe3+, the PL intensity of the N-CDs decreased gradually, revealing the PL intensity of the mixture was sensitive to Fe3+. The PL intensity at 450 nm displayed a good linear relationship versus the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–150 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.995 (Fig. 5b). The limit of detection was estimated to be 0.5 μM based on three times the standard deviation rule, which was lower than the maximum tolerance (5.4 μM) allowed by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in drinking water [50,51].

Selectivity is a critical parameter to evaluate the performance of a fluorescent chemosensor. We further evaluated the selectivity of the N-CDs as a fluorescent probe toward the potential interfering ions. The fluorescence response of the N-CDs in the presence of different metal ions at the concentration of 100 μM was analyzed (Fig. 5c), and the detailed PL spectra of the N-CDs were given in Fig. S8. As demonstrated in Fig. 5c, a significant fluorescence quenching effect was observed with the emergence of 100 μM Fe3+, while the PL intensity of the N-CDs was little affected in the presence of any other metal ions, including Na+, K+, Cr(VI), Zn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mn2+. Under identical concentration and parallel testing conditions, the N-CDs show excellent selectivity toward Fe3+ over other tested metal ions.

For a better understanding of the fluorescence quenching process of the N-CDs, the fluorescence decay measurements were performed. As shown in Fig. 5d, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs is measured to be 6.59 ns (the blue line), with excitation and emission wavelengths of 360 and 450 nm, respectively. After the presence of Fe3+, the lifetime of N-CDs decreases to 3.77 ns (the yellow line) accompanied by remarkable fluorescence quenching, implying the fast charge transfer between N-CDs and Fe3+. Due to the coordination interaction between N-CDs and Fe3+, electrons in the excited state of the N-CDs would transfer to the unfilled orbital of Fe3+ and lead to nonradiative recombination, which results in the fluorescence quenching [52,53]. It can be concluded that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs during Fe3+ detection is due to the charge transfer between N-CDs and Fe3+, which had been widely adopted for other probes to determine Fe3+ ions [15,54,55].

In addition, since CDs have demonstrated superior concentration-dependent emission, high stability and good biocompatibility, as-prepared N-CDs are proposed to serve as a potentially multicolor bio-labeling reagent. Herein, before using N-CDs for cell imaging, the cellular toxicity and imaging tests are preliminarily examined. The cytotoxicity of the N-CDs is evaluated by a standard MTT assay with HeLa cells. As shown in Fig. S9, over 85% cell viabilities are observed after incubation of HeLa cells with the N-CDs at the concentrations ranged from 10 to 100 mg/mL for 12 h, confirming low cytotoxicity of this material. Then, to reveal potential applications of these N-CDs in multiplexed bioimaging, their cellular imaging capability was preliminarily investigated. After incubating HeLa cells with each of N-CDs at different concentrations for 4 h, the live cells were imaged with a confocal microscope under the excitation light of 405 nm (Fig. 6 a and b) and 488 nm (Fig. 6c), respectively. The confocal micrographs show different colors of emission in HeLa cells (Fig. 6), demonstrating the N-CDs could potentially be employed in bio-labeling through endocytosis process. It is worthy to note from these figures that the emissions locate nearly at the whole cellular regions, suggesting the N-CDs not only can pass through cell membranes, but even enter into cell nuclei (Fig. 6 and Fig. S10). This result demonstrated N-CDs could be used as a probe for real-time optical cellular imaging especially as a probe for DNA gene expression.

In conclusion, the N-CDs have been prepared by a rapid and low-cost microwave pyrolysis method with citric acid and ammonia as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The obtained N-CDs exhibit a strong blue emission with quantum yield up to 44.3%, owing to fluorescence enhancement effect of introduced N atoms into carbon dots. Moreover, the obtained N-CDs can be used for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe (III), which is attributed to the nonradiative charge transfer caused by the coordination between N-CDs and Fe3+. Significantly, the resultant N-CDs display concentration-dependent emission, which means the emission color from blue to red can be achieved simply by increasing the concentration of the N-CDs aqueous solution. The huge red-shift in the emission peak from blue region to red region is believed to be due to the increase in the interparticle distance. The property of concentration-dependent emission may bring us a bright prospect in practical application of multicolor cellular imaging.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51502115), Public Health Research Center at Jiangnan University (No. JUPH201506), open project of state key laboratory of supramolecular structure and materials (sklssm201819), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (NO. JUSRP11708).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.03.021.
